questions.scenario: A canary at 10 percent produces 6 percent checkout errors. Stable remains healthy, still serves 90 percent of traffic, and can read every row written during the canary. Which action best protects users while preserving evidence?

options.text: Keep the canary running until the root cause is proven.
Set canary traffic to zero, record the signals, and investigate after recovery.
Hold the canary at 10 percent while collecting enough traces to confirm the release is the cause.
Reduce the canary to 1 percent, then prepare a hotfix while retaining a small production sample.

questions.explanation: The previous version is healthy and data-compatible, so traffic rollback is the fastest safe recovery. Recording the release evidence preserves diagnosis. Waiting or increasing exposure extends harm, while an unproven hotfix is slower than the ready recovery path. See [AWS CodeDeploy rollback and redeployment](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/latest/userguide/deployments-rollback-and-redeploy.html).

questions.scenario: Version 1.8.4 renamed a production column in place and has already processed orders. Version 1.8.3 still reads the removed column. A separate code defect now causes payment timeouts. Which recovery path is most defensible?

options.text: Roll the application back first and repair the database only if it fails.
Scale version 1.8.3 beside 1.8.4 to compare payment latency.
Restore the previous application and roll the database back to its pre-release snapshot before replaying accepted orders.
Use a narrow roll-forward or planned database recovery because the old application cannot run safely against current state.

questions.explanation: The destructive schema change removed the application's rollback path. Restoring old code without restoring compatible data can cause another outage. A focused roll-forward or explicit database recovery is safer than assuming image age implies compatibility. See [Kubernetes Deployment rollback](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/deployment/#rolling-back-a-deployment).

questions.scenario: Logs show that the release reads PAYMENTS_TIMEOUT_MS, but production defines PAYMENT_TIMEOUT_MS. The existing production variable name is also consumed by two stable services and cannot change during the incident. The application patch is a one-line name correction, CI and deployment take six minutes, and rollback would take 20 minutes because the old environment has been scaled down. What should the incident lead choose?

options.text: Roll forward with the reviewed fix through the fastest safe validation gate.
Roll back first because the previous artifact is known, even though that path takes 20 minutes.
Change the production variable to the name expected by the new release without creating a reviewed code change.
Pause the release and complete the normal full regression suite before applying the one-line correction.

questions.explanation: The cause is confirmed, the patch is narrow, and the measured recovery path is faster than rollback. Roll-forward still needs review and a release gate. A blanket rollback rule ignores recovery time, while trial-and-error config changes reduce confidence. See [Google SRE incident response](https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/).

questions.scenario: Checkout success drops while payment provider latency and database write latency also rise. The canary is scheduled to increase from 10 to 25 percent in two minutes, but the team cannot yet attribute the failure. What is the best immediate decision?

options.text: Hold at 10 percent and keep the current investigation informal until one dependency is proven responsible.
Return application traffic to stable immediately, then determine whether current database state is compatible.
Pause promotion, limit exposure, assign an owner, and set the next evidence-based decision time.
Disable the canary's payment path but continue increasing traffic to its other endpoints.

questions.explanation: Uncertainty is a reason to stop spreading the change, not to continue automatically. A pause preserves options while owners compare release and dependency evidence. Blind rollback or patching can add failure modes before compatibility and cause are known. See [Google SRE incident response](https://sre.google/workbook/incident-response/).

questions.scenario: A new producer has published queue events with a required promotionId field. Several old consumers reject unknown required fields, and the events cannot be removed from the queue. Which fact most strongly argues against an application rollback?

options.text: The previous container image is still present in the registry.
The previous consumers cannot process state already created by the failed release.
The previous consumers passed their last compatibility suite against the old event schema.
The producer can be rolled back before it publishes any additional incompatible events.

questions.explanation: Rollback safety depends on current data and message state, not just artifact availability. Old consumers that cannot understand queued events may fail immediately after rollback. Version numbers and queue capacity do not repair the contract mismatch. See [GitLab multi-version compatibility guidance](https://docs.gitlab.com/development/multi_version_compatibility/).

questions.scenario: A failed checkout release is affecting users, and the incident lead must choose a recovery path within five minutes. Which conditions make application rollback the strongest first action?

options.text: The previous immutable artifact or environment is still available.
The failed release has made irreversible external changes that old code cannot reconcile.
The previous version can operate against the current database and message state.
The team prefers rollback because it avoids documenting a decision.
Evidence indicates the new release introduced the user-facing failure.

questions.explanation: Rollback is strongest when there is a ready target, current state remains compatible, and removing the new release is likely to remove the incident. Irreversible state argues against rollback, and recovery choices still need an audit trail. See [AWS CodeDeploy rollback and redeployment](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy/latest/userguide/deployments-rollback-and-redeploy.html).
